title: ξαίνω
content:
ξαίνω

ξαίνω

48.1 · Activities Involving Cloth

to comb wool to comb wool as an initial step in the making of wool thread1 οὐ ξαίνουσιν οὐδὲ νήθουσιν οὐδὲ κοπιῶσιν ‘they do not comb wool, spin, or work hard’ MAT 6:28 (apparatus)

MAT 6:28

1 In MAT 6:28 the Sinaiticus manuscript has an interesting reading, οὐ ξένουσιν (= οὐ ξαίνουσιν), thus providing three processes or activities involving cloth rather than two. However, in view of the relatively limited support for this reading, most translators follow the text which has αὐξάνουσιν they grow.

title: ξενία
content:
ξενία

ξενία (G3578)

noun | -ας | ξενίζω

7.31 · Constructions · Parts and Areas of Buildings

guestroom, lodging for guest, place to stay in a place of temporary lodging for a person away from home

ACT 28:231 · PHM 1:221

34.57 · Association · Show Hospitality

hospitality hospitality to a stranger, that is, someone who is not regarded as a member of the extended family or a close friend To receive a stranger as a guest is sometimes expressed idiomatically as ‘to let a stranger sit at one’s table,’ ‘to offer a bed to a stranger,’ or ‘to let a stranger enter one’s house.’
For another interpretation of ξενία in PHM 1:22, see 7.31.

PHM 1:221

1 ACT 28:23 · PHM 1:22 It is also possible to interpret ξενία in both ACT 28:23 and PHM 1:22 as hospitality or the activity involved in receiving a guest (see 34.57). This is the more common meaning of ξενία, but it fits less well in the two contexts in which ξενία occurs in the NT.

title: ξενίζω
content:
ξενίζω

ξενίζω (G3579) — ξενίζομαι

ξενία · ξενοδοχέω · ξένος · φιλοξενία · φιλόξενος

34.57 · Association · Show Hospitality

to show hospitality, to receive a stranger as a guest, hospitality to receive and show hospitality to a stranger, that is, someone who is not regarded as a member of the extended family or a close friend To receive a stranger as a guest is sometimes expressed idiomatically as ‘to let a stranger sit at one’s table,’ ‘to offer a bed to a stranger,’ or ‘to let a stranger enter one’s house.’

ACT 10:6 · ACT 10:18 · ACT 10:23 · ACT 10:32 · ACT 21:16 · ACT 28:7 · HEB 13:2

25.206 · (ξενίζομαι) · Attitudes and Emotions · Surprise, Astonish

to be surprised (compare ξένος ‘unknown, surprising,’ 28.34) to experience a sudden feeling of unexpected wonder The experience of ‘surprise’ may be expressed idiomatically in some languages, for example, ‘to be left with one’s mouth open’ or ‘to have one’s eyes made wide open.’

ACT 17:20 · 1PE 4:4 · 1PE 4:12

title: ξενοδοχέω
content:
ξενοδοχέω

ξενοδοχέω (G3580)

ξενίζω + δέχομαι

34.57 · Association · Show Hospitality

to show hospitality, to receive a stranger as a guest, hospitality to receive and show hospitality to a stranger, that is, someone who is not regarded as a member of the extended family or a close friend To receive a stranger as a guest is sometimes expressed idiomatically as ‘to let a stranger sit at one’s table,’ ‘to offer a bed to a stranger,’ or ‘to let a stranger enter one’s house.’

1TI 5:10

title: ξένος
content:
ξένος

ξένος (G3581)

adj | -η, ον | ξενίζω

28.34 · Know · Well Known, Clearly Shown, Revealed

unknown, unheard of, unfamiliar, surprising pertaining to not being previously known and hence unheard of and unfamiliar

MAT 27:7 · ACT 17:18 · HEB 13:9 · 1PE 4:12

adj | -ου | ξενίζω

11.73 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio-Political

stranger, foreigner a person belonging to a socio-political group other than the reference group Terms for ‘stranger’ or ‘foreigner’ are often based upon geographical differences or upon lack of previous knowledge. For example, ‘I was a stranger’ may be rendered as ‘I came from another country’ or ‘I was not known to any of you.’

MAT 25:35 · MAT 25:38 · MAT 25:43 · MAT 25:44 · ACT 17:21 · EPH 2:12 · EPH 2:19 · HEB 11:13 · 3JN 1:5

34.60 · ((ου m)) · Association · Show Hospitality

host a person who shows hospitality to guests In rendering ‘host’ it may be necessary to employ a phrase, for example, ‘who welcomed and took care of us in his home.’

ROM 16:23

title: ξέστης
content:
ξέστης

ξέστης (G3582)

noun | -ου

6.126 · Artifacts · Containers

pitcher, jar (a borrowing based on Latin sextarius, a Roman measure equal to about a half liter, one pint) a kind of small pitcher or jar

MRK 7:4

title: ξηραίνω
content:
ξηραίνω

ξηραίνω (G3583) — ξηραίνομαι

aor. ἐξήρανα · aor.pass. ἐξηράνθην · pf.pass. ἐξήραμμαι | ξηρός

79.82 · Features of Objects · Wet, Dry

to dry out, to dry up, to wither to cause something to become dry

MAT 13:6 · MAT 21:19 · MAT 21:20 · LUK 8:6 · JAS 1:11 · 1PE 1:24

79.81 · (ξηραίνομαι[a]) · Features of Objects · Wet, Dry

to dry up (derivative of ξηρός[a] ‘dry,’ 79.80) to become dry It may be more appropriate in a number of languages to translate this clause in MRK 5:29 as ‘and immediately her menstrual bleeding stopped.’

MRK 4:6 · MRK 5:29 · MRK 11:20 · MRK 11:21 · JHN 15:6 · REV 16:12

23.198 · (ξηραίνομαι[b]) · Physiological Processes and States · Ripen, Produce Fruit, Bear Seed

to become ripe, to be ripe (derivative of ξηρός[a] ‘dry,’ 79.80) to become ripe, with reference to grain which dries in the process of ripening

REV 14:15

23.172 · (ξηραίνομαι[c]) · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to be stiff, to be paralyzed (a figurative extension of meaning of ξηραίνομαι[a] ‘to dry up,’ 79.81) to become stiff to the point of not being able to move In some languages one may express the meaning of ‘to be stiff all over’ as ‘not able to move any part of the body.’

MRK 3:1 · MRK 9:18

title: ξηρός
content:
ξηρός

ξηρός (G3584) — ξηρά

adj | -ά, -όν | ξηραίνω

79.80 · Features of Objects · Wet, Dry

dry, withered pertaining to being dry

LUK 23:31 · HEB 11:29

23.173 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

withered, paralyzed (a figurative extension of meaning of ξηρός[a] ‘dry, withered,’ 79.80) pertaining to a shrunken, withered, and hence immobile part of the body

MAT 12:10 · MRK 3:3 · LUK 6:6 · LUK 6:8 · JHN 5:3

1.60 · (ξηρά (ᾶς f)) · Geographical Objects and Features · Land in Contrast With the Sea

land dry land, in contrast with the sea In a number of languages a clear distinction is made between terms which designate land in contrast with water and those which designate cultivated land. Even in English the normal term for land close to water is ‘shore’ or ‘coast,’ and therefore one may translate MAT 14:24 as ‘the boat was already far away from the shore.’ Similarly, in ACT 27:27 one may translate ‘we were getting close to shore.’

MAT 23:15 · HEB 11:29

title: ξύλινος
content:
ξύλινος

ξύλινος (G3585)

adj | -η, -ον | ξύλον

3.62 · Plants · Wood and Wood Products

of wood, wooden (derivative of ξύλον[a] ‘wood,’ 3.60) pertaining to being made or consisting of wood

2TI 2:20 · REV 9:20

title: ξύλον
content:
ξύλον

ξύλον (G3586)

noun | -ου | ξύλινος

3.60 · Plants · Wood and Wood Products

wood wood as a substance A number of languages make a clear distinction between wood as a substance used in artifacts and constructions and wood used for heating or cooking. A further distinction may be made between durable wood (not readily subject to rot or to the effects of termites and borers) and less durable and therefore relatively unsatisfactory wood.

1CO 3:12 · REV 18:12 · REV 18:12

3.61 · Plants · Wood and Wood Products

firewood wood as a substance used to burn for heat ξύλον in LUK 23:31 may also be interpreted as a live green tree (see 3.4). If one assumes that the meaning here is ‘firewood,’ then it may be necessary to translate ‘wood which has not had an opportunity to dry out’ or ‘wood which is still green’ or ‘... which is still wet with sap.’

LUK 23:31

3.4 · Plants · Trees

tree large perennial plant, usually having a woody central stem, called a trunk, with secondary branches growing from it It is also possible to understand ξύλον in LUK 23:31 as ‘firewood.’ One may therefore translate the first part of this conditional sentence as ‘if they do these things to firewood which has recently been cut’ or ‘if they do these things to firewood which hasn’t dried out’ (see 3.61).
In REV 2:7 and REV 22:2 ξύλον τῆς ζωῆς ‘tree of life’ may cause certain complications if the passage is translated more or less literally ‘I will give him to eat of the tree of life.’ In some languages a literal rendering would mean ‘to gnaw on the tree of life,’ that is to say, to eat the wood or the bark. It may therefore be necessary to translate ‘to eat the fruit of the tree of life’ or ‘... the fruit from the tree that causes life’ or ‘... causes people to live.’

LUK 23:31 · GAL 3:13 · REV 2:7 · REV 22:2 · REV 22:2 · REV 22:14 · REV 22:19

6.31 · Artifacts · Weapons and Armor

club a heavy stick used in fighting

MAT 26:47 · MAT 26:55 · MRK 14:43 · MRK 14:48 · LUK 22:52

6.21 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Binding and Fastening

stocks an instrument consisting of heavy blocks of wood through which the legs were placed and then securely fastened Stocks were employed primarily as an instrument for imprisonment, but also as a means of punishment.

ACT 16:24

6.28 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Punishment and Execution

cross (a figurative extension of meaning of ξύλον[a] ‘wood,’ 3.60) an instrument of execution Outside the NT ξύλον[f] often refers to a gallows, but in the NT contexts speaking of capital punishment, the reference is to the cross on which Jesus was crucified.

ACT 5:30 · ACT 10:39 · ACT 13:29 · 1PE 2:24

title: ξυράω
content:
ξυράω

ξυράω (G3587) — ξυράομαι

19.24 · Physical Impact · Pierce, Cut

to shave to shave the head or beard

ACT 21:24 · 1CO 11:5 · 1CO 11:6
